speaker: interviewer
text: --survivor Abraham Melezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. So here comes Thursday, October the 2nd. And I am in the yard with-- I don't know-- a couple thousands of Jews, maybe more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the night was rather cold, you know. Vilna is much, much more to the north than in New York, you know, and higher of about a thousand feet above--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sea level.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sea level. So it was cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what to do? And some started out-- there were some very pious Jews. And they started, you know, to say their prayers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I sat. And I thought-- I thought whether I would see Sula and Zarek again, what they were doing, and so on. And if they would take us-- I know a regular barrack at Ponary. So what to do in case? Let myself shoot-- the way they caught me, or just to, to, to let-- be shot, fighting.

speaker: interviewee
text: That moment, the gate opens, and comes in a German. He seems to be  a sargent, you know. I think that his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I am not sure-- with a list.

speaker: interviewee
text: And started-- it was about 10 o'clock. We were still there-- and started to call out names. How do you say? You know, in German, Z is read like 'tse,' 'Me-letse-in.' He called out, Melezin! And he said, Abraham Melezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I jumped up. And come here. I didn't know what. He called, called out one more name. I don't remember who was it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] According to me, it was Zarek. You know-- and took out us to other yard. The camp was surrounded by the prison walls and buildings. And through these-- those grates, Jewish women with children, what-- you know, they knew what's happening to them. Asked not to be-- to be liberated, to give their names during the night, you know. Later, I learned it from Sula-- during the night, you know, some of them committed suicide, having [INAUDIBLE]. Some others took their jewelry and their-- and put it into the toilets and let it run down, you know, with-- into the toilets.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said-- so the German went our ways there told me that your name is on the list as a very important Arbeiter in the commander so-and-so. And you have a pass. Here, yes I have a pass. I pulled out my pass. You have a wife? Yes, I said, a wife. I gave the name of my wife. So he called-- go-- went in and called out Sula. And so they called out about 70 people, about-- half men, half women, and some children. And they led us from the Lukiszki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right at the gate of the prison was-- I saw on the other side of the street, Mrs. Rewenska. We could all-- everybody knew that Melezin was caught because the-- Antony, the janitor, and Mrs. Rewenska, and others already knew. As soon as Sula-- when Sula saw me with Zarek, she jumped to me, grabbed the child, and ran away, ran away. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I-- and we back to ghetto. When I came back to [? bed, ?] I saw among the policemen were some students of mine, you know. So they said, well, we're so happy to see you. Maybe we can help, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, you son of bitches, get out! You want to help me to put me back in the ghetto? First, you came, you know, to call me out of the ghetto, and so on? I said, and now look, you better-- the best thing what you can do-- disappear from the ghetto before they'll disappear you, you know. They'll do in you, so to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, we came back. I had no illusion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It means first of all, I decide I'm so close to the end of the ghetto, like anything that happens, organized, or disorganized, or accidental-- so I'm too close to it, you know. And I, I sort of-- I said, Sula, we have to get out from the ghetto. I said, I with my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] face-- and Sula was, you know-- had olive complexion. She was brunette with dark eyes, you know, a typical Mideastern type, you know. In Italy she would go off as a southern Italian or a Greek women, you know. But in Vilna, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] So I said, Sula, regardless, let's go in hiding. I'll talk to Rewenska and Antony. Let's see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, where is Zarek? What's with-- so I said, we don't want Zarek. When she grab side, told her right away, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The next day, I went-- and it was Friday, October the 3rd. I went with commander to [NON-ENGLISH]. Now, we have to cross the River Vilnia over so-called Green Bridge. And here, on the path was Antony. I said, Antony, tell Mrs. Rewenska that I want leave the ghetto. And give me proper instructions on the way back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way back-- I make sure to tell him on the side, proper side-- here is Anthony. And he started to give me instructions. First, he said, send out Sula from the ghetto and tell later me where to wait for her. And try to work in the same place in [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came Friday night from the work and pulled out some money. I had sewn in money-- sewn in. And I started to work in order to organize to take out Sula from the ghetto because only those who had passes could out from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I organized. It cost some money. And I organized. I whisked-- she was whisked out. Where was your son during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: So she will be taken out with one of commander who gets out and takes out-- I don't know-- empty, empty bags and brings back food or whatever it is, you know. Anyway, she was put-- next day, she was put on the truck and covered with bags and gone. In the evening, still [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came up suddenly a man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he says, we are-- we need money because we want to make a present to the German. So I said, at the moment, I don't have money here. I would need at least a day or so. I would know tomorrow morning, early morning. Meantime, I learned probably-- I don't remember-- but probably, I learned from Rachela that she has given my name to that German, who pulled me out because Rachela was taken out by the [NON-ENGLISH], the mafia, the Jewish mafia, you know. And in the morning, she ran to the work place. She worked in a-- in a hotel for-- in a former academic house, a dormitory, which was turned into officer hotel, you know. She used to clean the-- I don't know, wash the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] windows, or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told me that's how she-- how she-- right in the morning, she went to work. And I was ready to go, too, with my commander, because I have arranged Sula-- comes in that guy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, who gave my name? He said, I gave. He said, I have given your name because your students, a policeman ask me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how is the name of the [GERMAN]? And he said some kind of a name. I didn't know. I said, you're trying to bilk out for your money, you know. And I got mad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whatever I could-- and with-- I gave him a punch, you know. I hit him so hard that he simply rolled down. And I went out. I left him there in the room. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All right, it was October the 5th. Sula was already on the truck under the bags. And I went with the commander to work [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There, I met those soviet POWs, you know, prisoners, and-- that started with that Jewish soldier, who tried to ask him to be safe, and whatever or not. It was a gray day. It drizzled. About 2:00 PM, suddenly, somebody called me out. Melezin, Melezin, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I see here is another SS man who has a revolver. Come. You know, I didn't know what happened. He took me to this gate, showed some kind of a paper to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the guard-- probably showed it also when he came in. And there in front, was a cab-- horse-drawn cab, who is another soldier, you know, in front. And the, the canopy of the cab was up because it was drizzling. And he goes, come in here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I came in. And he went-- sat in the front as the second one. I turned around. There was some kind of a lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see that the Pole-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I couldn't-- I looked in. What happened? And so she says-- she says to me, Dr. Melezin? I said, yes. All right, let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they drove in that-- hiding with-- under that canopy, with two crazy soldiers in front. And we drove to [NON-ENGLISH] Street all the way up. And then they took me-- then they stopped. Both get up, start to look around. And then in the moment, one opened the door to a house. It was a three-story house or four-story house. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then suddenly, get out. Run! So I run in-- into the-- into the foyer. In a moment, this lady came out. I have somewhere written her name. I don't remember. And they took me on the third floor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, yes? And she's telling me, your wife was met and taken off. And she's in hiding in proper place. Was that with your son, Mr.-- Dr. Melezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this was Sunday--

speaker: interviewer
text: With your-- with your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: --October 5. She told me my wife. I didn't know anything about my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Then she introduced herself, whatever or not. I said, all right, I said. You probably know that it's-- we are an organization, you know, probably from Wanda. It's Wanda Rewenska. Yes, I have heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Wanda made me understand, once, that Poles organized something. But, you know that to deal with the Jews is very dangerous. I said, aren't you afraid? She said, what if you were-- if you will be afraid, you won't do anything, you know. Who are these two boys? Oh, it-- he-- university student. One is-- was in the law school. And the other was in the department for agriculture. They've known you? I said, yes, I would. The law student doesn't know. But the department for agriculture does know you, does know. But he's-- he's safe. He-- he's a safe man.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, October 6 and 7, I was in hiding in this apartment. First of all, during the war, you know, on Sunday, the Einsatz commander, they beat us up. And what they did, you know, they put down the bayonets. And they used-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I had somewhere here, right over my anus, you know. And the evening came. I forgot the name. It's a very well known Polish doctor, a woman. And she made the dressing there, looked at other things, my black marks, whatever it is. Next day, I was there. Some kind of a man came in. Later, I suspected that this was the commandant of the AK, of the Home Army of Vilna. I suspected he's known the girl, who was Wilk. It means 'wolf.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also came Rewenska [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and told me that she would prepare for me-- she thought to brief me-- that she would prepare for me a birth certificate from a, a parish in Warsaw, which was bombed out and all the secretariat burned. Therefore, it is safe to give out-- yeah. They have all prepared these all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she told me, look, I want you should go with your wife and-- to Lida and organize there an AK unit in the district Lida West. And I said-- I said, I remember, I go Dr. Wanda, you want to put me on top to organize?

speaker: interviewee
text: With your posture and your looks, you don't look to me like a Jew. And beside that, you, you know very well. You're a lieutenant of Polish-- of Reserve.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, and so what? I, I should organize Poles? Give-- I can do anything, you know, some kinds of-- So she said, said, Dr. Melezin, Abraham, be realistic. If you won't be on top, you won't survive on the bottom even a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then Antony-- on Tuesday, 8th of October, Antony came. He brought me entirely different outfit, you know. He brought for me a-- a railroad workers had the uniform-- a blue jacket, closed up-- all the way up, blue trousers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he brought me this kind of outfit, and, and different cap. But I had my over-jacket with my own money sewn in there, you know. So this remained. It was about 3/4, you know, grey-- dark-grey color.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this afternoon, on Tuesday, October 8, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was already dressed as a former rail worker, you know, came up again a cab. It was a regular-- I looked, you know-- just a, a driver. And I and our future guide, by the name Josef, a very good-looking man, tall-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and took us. And we went to the cab. And we went into an entirely different part of the city. Here, I was in Polanka very high, you know, near university. And he took us all the way across the city to southeastern part of the city through [NON-ENGLISH] Street near the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the suburbia called [NON-ENGLISH]. And from there, started the highway to Lida. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- he dropped us there. I found there Wanda Rewenska with the passport. I said-- all right, I said. I said, Mrs. Wanda, I say that I should make-- I said, what I should pay? I should make contribution.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, look, I don't do it for you. It is not for money, for you, you know, because you are now a member of AK. And your rank is much higher than your official army rank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what AK is?

speaker: interviewee
text: AK, it is the Armia Krajowa, 'Home Army.'

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the rightist nationalist organization. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Toward the evening came Sula, Iuzia with Zarek. And Iuzia start to tell me that I should leave the child here with her. And she will go to the village. And she would hide there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said-- and we started to argue. Sula-- it was very tempting, you know. But then I said, look, I said, don't you know he is circumscribed? How? So she agreed. So we took the child. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is how-- on October 9, on the Wednesday. It was Wednesday. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a peasant wagon. We took some-- Iuzia brought some certain things, let's say, my winter coat, Sula's-- what called it-- not lamb, you know, Persian lamb coat, other-- a lot of things, you know. And we start our trek to Lida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name of that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lida, L-I-D-A. It was about 60 miles south of Vilna, 95 kilometers. And with us was Josef, you know, that guide. So we were in-- on the road. We were the 10th, 11th, 12th, and 13th, Sunday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to sleep in various places, so-called safe houses. And we started on the main-- on the main road. But then we went, you know, to side roads. And then we cross from one-- from, from the left side of the road, from eastern side of the road to the western side of the road, and again, over zigzagging, you know. It is how it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Sunday, October 13th, we came to Lida. He dropped-- let me tell you one thing, that on the one, one night, it was the second night that Josef told me, I want from you $500. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look, I said, you were-- you were there when I offered make-- made-- make a contribution, and Rewenska didn't take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you want from an AK member take money? I don't have $500 with me. He said, you should have some money. Don't tell me. I said, I wouldn't be if I won't give you. I said, I, I, I can't leave us without money. Oh, so then-- I take away the wagon, go, all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I was a very-- at the time, quite violent, you know. I didn't know what to hit him, to tell him [NON-ENGLISH] means 'attention' in-- whatever it is. Then Sula came up. She took out from under somewhere, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a ring with a diamond stone. Look, I said. He said that he has no money. And it is true. But I have-- I saved this. Take it. He took it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, it was a diamond with rubies around. It was about-- but it was more than a karat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you arrived in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Lida, Dr. Melezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he dropped us. We went in. And then I started to see, you know. And I know this street because already, we did some research in this area. So I know this city was the main street, which entrance to the Lida. On both sides were houses. Behind were, I remember, were psychiatric hospital. And he looked. And he stopped at one house. We'll occupy-- you will occupy this house. I went. He said, this is a, a house after Jews. It's why-- it's why it's empty. So they won't return here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went into the house. And he disappeared. Now, I knew already whom I have to contact in Lida, you know, everything, of course, [POINTS] in the head. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was about a kitchen and four rooms. It's what, I may say, one large room in front, and then-- and this is what you may say, three bedrooms-- one larger one, two smaller one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Outhouse in the backyard on the right corner of that. And in the left corner, was a well. So I could crank water, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started to organize. Next day, we try to-- I inspected the house, organize. We kept, you know, Sula inside. And, of course, she was, you know, dressed like a peasant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we kept the, the child inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found in the house a sheepskin, you know, crudely made. In Polish, it's called [POLISH]. Probably, it was one of the proprietors of the house. But this was-- I look at it, and I say, I tried it. And it was good on me. And I said, this is very important for winter, you know. I put-- so I looked like-- I looked like a peasant, or whatever it is. There is some furniture. We organize it. And we stayed probably for a day or so in the house at Monday.
